insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles verified purchase events at roughly $0.10 each — a 50–200x cost gap driven by the receipt itself serving as the conversion event, not a modeled inference.

Cookies are dead, device fingerprinting is degrading, and first-party data stops at the brand's own ecosystem edge — none of these produce identity-free, cryptographically verified purchase signals across vendors, which is the specific gap this dataset fills.

17 active CBSAs show a rural-first expansion pattern across 11+ non-metro state designations — a geographic profile that is structurally absent from legacy ad tech data, which skews heavily toward metro device density.

The dataset already spans King Soopers, Walmart, Kroger, Amazon, Target, Costco, and Home Depot without requiring cooperation from any of them — aggregate verified spend across competing retailers in a single queryable layer does not exist elsewhere.

Five SaaS vendor requests (Notion, Render, Vercel, DigitalOcean, Hetzner) are filed but unactivated; if they clear, physical retail and digital subscription spend become linkable in the same verified dataset for the first time.

One active brand predicate confirms that at least one brand has moved from awareness to querying verified purchase history against this dataset — the demand signal exists, but predicate lifecycle data is not yet visible, so durability is unconfirmed.
